question: A 70 kg astronaut travels to the Moon where $g_{Moon} = 1.62\,\text{m/s}^2$. What is their mass on the Moon?

options.text: 70 kg
420 kg

hint: Mass is an intrinsic property, independent of gravitational field. See the Motivation section.
explanation: Mass is always 70 kg regardless of location. Only weight changes with gravity. Weight on Moon = 70 × 1.62 = 113.4 N, compared to 686 N on Earth.

question: What is the weight of a 5 kg object on Earth ($g = 9.8\,\text{m/s}^2$)?

options.text: 5 N

hint: Weight = mg. See the Summary section.
explanation: W = mg = 5 × 9.8 = 49 N. Weight is the gravitational force on the mass.

question: Which best describes the difference between inertial mass and gravitational mass?

options.text: Inertial mass is larger on Earth; gravitational mass on the Moon
Inertial mass measures resistance to acceleration; gravitational mass determines gravitational force
They are completely different quantities with no known relationship
Inertial mass is in kg; gravitational mass is in Newtons

hint: The equivalence principle says they are numerically equal. See the Derivation section.
explanation: Inertial mass (m in F=ma) resists acceleration. Gravitational mass (m in F=GMm/r²) determines gravitational interaction. They are experimentally equal to extraordinary precision — the equivalence principle.

question: Why do a feather and a hammer fall at the same rate in a vacuum?

options.text: Gravity ignores mass in a vacuum
The equivalence of inertial and gravitational mass causes mass to cancel in free-fall acceleration
Vacuum removes the difference in air resistance
All objects have the same mass in vacuum

hint: Trace the algebra: F_g = mg, a = F/m. See the Derivation section.
explanation: F_gravity = m_grav × g. a = F_gravity / m_inertial = (m_grav / m_inertial) × g. Since m_grav = m_inertial, a = g for every object. Mass cancels, making free-fall acceleration universal.

question: Which scenario best illustrates inertia (not weight)?

options.text: Lifting a heavy box from the floor
Pushing a heavy box to get it moving on a frictionless surface
Feeling heavy in an accelerating elevator
Weighing yourself on a scale

hint: Inertia is resistance to changes in velocity; weight is the gravitational force. See the Intuition section.
explanation: Pushing the box to accelerate it on a frictionless surface only involves inertia (resistance to acceleration), not weight (which only matters if you're lifting against gravity). The same force gives less acceleration to a more massive box regardless of gravity.

question: Write the weight $W$ of an object with mass $m$ in a gravitational field with acceleration $g$.
hint: Weight is the gravitational force on a mass. See the Summary section.
explanation: W = mg. Weight is a force (in Newtons), while mass is in kg. They are proportional on the surface of any body, but only equal numerically when g = 1 (which is not the case on Earth).

question: An unknown object is pushed by force $F$ and accelerates at $a$. Write its mass $m$.
hint: Newton's second law defines inertial mass operationally. See the Derivation section.
explanation: From F = ma: m = F/a. This is the operational definition of inertial mass — apply a known force, measure the acceleration.

question: Match each concept to its correct description:
pairs: Intrinsic scalar property; measures inertia; constant regardless of location
Gravitational force on a mass: W = mg; depends on local g
Resistance to acceleration; appears in F = ma
Determines gravitational attraction; appears in F = GMm/r²
hint: Review the Derivation and Summary sections for precise definitions.
explanation: These distinctions are fundamental. Mass is intrinsic; weight depends on gravity. Inertial and gravitational mass are the same value experimentally — the equivalence principle.

question: An object floats in space (zero gravity). To assess its mass, you should:

options.text: Weigh it on a scale
Apply a known force and measure its acceleration
It has no mass in zero gravity
Count its atoms

hint: Inertial mass can be measured without gravity. See the Intuition section.
explanation: Without gravity, scales don't work. But F = ma still holds: apply a known force and measure the resulting acceleration. m = F/a gives the inertial mass. This is how mass is measured on the ISS.

question: Match each statement to whether it is about mass or weight:
pairs: Unchanged when you travel to Mars
About one-third of Earth value on Mars
hint: Mass is intrinsic; weight depends on gravity. See the Misconceptions section.
explanation: Mass is always in kg and never changes with location. Weight = mg in Newtons and scales with the local gravitational field. On Mars (g ≈ 3.7 m/s²), weight is about 38% of Earth weight.
